PAD System Architecture Overview (Core Intake & Entry Workflow)

Introduction

This document describes the initial stages of the PAD workflow, including case intake, automated entry processing, and the beginning of the negotiation stage.

These steps represent the foundational processes of the PAD system. While they are not the entire lifecycle of a case, they are critical because all subsequent processes — including negotiation, arbitration preparation, and case management — depend on the data and validations performed during these stages.

Accurate data extraction and validation at this stage ensures the integrity of the entire arbitration pipeline.

⸻

Step 1: Case Intake

Cases enter the PAD CMS (Case Management System) through multiple intake channels. Regardless of the source, all incoming cases are eventually routed into the Entry Queue for processing.

Intake Sources

1. Client API Integration
Clients can submit cases directly through an API integration. These requests automatically create case entries in the PAD intake system and place them into the entry queue.

2. USMON Queue Retrieval
PAD automatically retrieves cases assigned to Callagy Recovery from the USMON system. These cases are pulled from the USMON queue and inserted into the PAD entry queue for processing.

3. Client Portal Uploads
Clients may upload cases through a web portal. These cases are first analyzed using AI to determine whether they are viable for pursuit. Once approved by the AI analysis, the cases are placed into the entry queue.

4. Spreadsheet Imports
Some clients provide case lists via spreadsheet files. These spreadsheets are processed and converted into individual case records, which are then added to the entry queue.

Step 2: Entry Queue Processing

All incoming cases are placed into a centralized Entry Queue.

Processing of the queue is handled by two Python processing services running on separate virtual machines (VMs). These services continuously monitor and process cases in the queue based on urgency.

Case Priority Rules

Cases are prioritized based on the time remaining before the 30-day deadline from the initial EOB date.

Priority levels include:

Hot Rush
1–4 days remaining before the 30-day deadline.

Rush
5–7 days remaining before the 30-day deadline.

On Time
More than 7 days remaining before the case becomes ineligible.

The system ensures that higher-priority cases are processed before lower-priority ones to minimize missed deadlines.

⸻

Automated System Entry

After data extraction, the system uses Selenium browser automation to log into PAD and perform the file entry process.

The automation process performs several validation checks before entering the case:
    •    Missing documentation
    •    Missing required fields
    •    Incomplete claim information

If required data is missing, the case is skipped and flagged for manual review.

If all required information is present, Selenium completes the automated file entry process.

Once the entry process is completed successfully, the case is automatically assigned the status:

Delivered

This status indicates that the case has been successfully entered into the PAD system and is ready for the next operational stage.

⸻

Step 3: Negotiation Preparation

After the case is entered and marked as Delivered, it proceeds to the Negotiation stage.

At this stage, staff must verify the accuracy of the data extracted by the AI system.

Although Gemini performs well in most scenarios, it can occasionally misidentify or incorrectly extract certain fields.

Common problem areas include:
    •    Claim number
    •    Provider name
    •    Insurance carrier name
    •    Treatment details

Treatment information can be complex and may include multiple structured data elements such as:
    •    CPT codes
    •    Modifiers
    •    Units
    •    Amount billed
    •    Copay
    •    Deductible
    •    Insurance payment
    •    QPA (Qualified Payment Amount)

Because of the complexity and importance of this information, human verification remains necessary before negotiation can proceed.

⸻

Negotiation Requirements

The negotiation process requires accurate claim information because this data is submitted directly to insurance carriers.

Negotiations must be completed within 30 days of the initial EOB date.

content.thinking: Danny is saying the highest-leverage improvement areas are: claim number, treatment details, insurance carrier name, and provider name. These are the four critical extraction fields. Let me engage thoughtfully on this.

content.text:
[[reply_to_current]] Those four fields make sense as the priority targets — they're also the four that cause the most downstream damage when wrong.

Let me think through each:

**Claim Number** — usually a structured alphanumeric string, but format varies wildly by carrier. The risk is Gemini grabbing a similar-looking number (authorization number, reference number) when the claim number isn't clearly labeled. A carrier-specific format library could help validate what was extracted.

**Insurance Carrier Name** — the BCBS problem you already described. This one is cleanest to solve: a normalized alias table maps every known variation to a canonical carrier ID. One-time build, ongoing maintenance as new variations appear.

**Provider Name** — similar to carrier, but the variation source is different. Providers might appear as the individual physician, the group practice, or the facility — all on the same document. The question is: which one does PAD need?

**Treatment Details** — this is the hardest. CPT codes, modifiers, units, QPA — these are often in tables inside the PDF, and table extraction is where OCR and AI both struggle most, especially on scanned documents.

**My question:** Of these four, which one is causing the most cases to fail out of automation right now — and which one, if fixed, would have the biggest impact on recovery outcomes?

That tells us where to put the engineering effort first. 🏥

content.thinking: Danny is proposing storing the raw Gemini-extracted names alongside the normalized/matched names in the database. This is a smart idea - it creates a dual-column approach: raw extracted value + matched canonical ID. Let me respond thoughtfully to this.

content.text:
[[reply_to_current]] That's a sharp idea — and it solves more than one problem at once.

**Storing raw extracted values gives you:**

1. **An audit trail** — when a match is wrong, you can see exactly what Gemini returned vs. what the system mapped it to. That's how you catch and correct systemic errors.

2. **Letter generation accuracy** — negotiation letters going to insurance carriers need to reference the carrier exactly as they appear on the EOB. "BCBS of Texas" in the letter, not just the internal canonical ID. Raw storage makes that automatic.

3. **A training dataset** — over time, the raw name columns become a corpus of real-world variations. You can mine that to continuously improve the alias mapping table without needing to manually discover every variation.

**The schema pattern I'd suggest:**
- `insurance_carrier_id` — matched canonical ID (existing)  
- `insurance_carrier_raw` — exactly as Gemini extracted it (new)
- `provider_id` — matched canonical ID (existing)
- `provider_raw` — exactly as Gemini extracted it (new)

Simple addition, significant upside. And it's non-destructive — you're not changing how the matching works, just preserving what came in before the match.

Does the current PAD database have a migration workflow, or would adding these columns require a more involved process on your end?

content.text:
[[reply_to_current]] Mark, this is the clearest articulation of the full system we've had yet. Let me confirm I have it right:

**The 4-Layer AI Architecture:**

**Layer 1 — Data Extraction & Validation**
AI ingests EOB + HCFI → extracts and verifies all case data (patient, treatment, eligibility, CPT codes, QPA, carrier, provider) → writes clean data to PAD database

**Layer 2 — Negotiation Trigger**
AI reviews validated data → confirms accuracy → triggers existing negotiation letter automation. No human in the loop for clean cases.

**Layer 3 — Filing Trigger**
AI reviews database state → triggers filing automation + existing email automations connected to filing. Same pattern — AI as the trigger, existing machinery executes.

**Layer 4 — Email Agent (the unlock)**
This is the right call and the most important insight in this message. The email inbox is where the entire case lifecycle actually lives between the major stages. Every carrier response, objection, dismissal, payment link, offer link — it all comes in as email. Right now a human reads it, decides what it means, and takes the next step.

The Email Agent flips that:
- Incoming email → AI classifies it (objection? dismissal? payment link? offer link? additional docs request?)
- AI matches it to the correct case
- AI triggers the appropriate next step directly — no human reads the email first

**This eliminates the real bottleneck.** The major stages (Intake → Negotiation → Filing → IDR → Resolution) are relatively clean. The chaos lives in the thousands of micro-decisions happening in email between those stages. That's where cases stall. That's where human capacity runs out.

One question to confirm scope: is the case email correspondence coming into a shared inbox (like cases@callagy.com), or is it distributed across individual staff emails right now? That determines how we architect the Email Agent's access. 🏥
